_comment: "Unbreakable Soul" (Mastered) — Vedic Metal × Indian Classical Fusion by Samskara Stream (channel @SamskaraStream). The piece weaves a SINGLE canonical anchor (Bhagavad Gītā 2.23, the famous 'weapons cannot cut it' verse on the indestructible Self) with contemporary Sanskrit composition by the artist, English verses, and a closing peace mantra. The contemporary Sanskrit verses are not from any canonical scripture — they're the songwriter's own composition in Vedantic vocabulary. The 'ajaro'ham amarā'ham' chorus likewise. Marked clearly in each explanation field. English-only verses (Pre-Chorus, Chorus English half, Bridge English half) are not included as lyrics_lines.
title: Unbreakable Soul — Vedic Metal Fusion (BG 2.23 + contemporary)

lines.devanagari: नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि नैनं दहति पावकः । न चैनं क्लेदयन्त्यापो न शोषयति मारुतः ॥
lines.iast: nainaṃ chindanti śastrāṇi nainaṃ dahati pāvakaḥ | na cainaṃ kledayanty āpo na śoṣayati mārutaḥ ||
lines.english_literal: Weapons do not cut this (Self); fire does not burn this; waters do not wet this; wind does not dry it.
lines.english_poetic: Weapons cannot cut it. Fire cannot burn it. Waters cannot wet it. Wind cannot dry it.
lines.explanation: Bhagavad Gītā 2.23 — Kṛṣṇa's teaching to Arjuna on the indestructible Self. One of the most quoted verses in the Gītā's second chapter (the Sāṃkhya-Yoga), where the case for the Self's eternity is made via the impossibility of harm from any element. The four elements named cover all material destruction: weapons (earth/metal), fire, water, wind. The Self is none of these — it is the witness behind them all.

lines.devanagari: शान्तोऽस्मि निर्विकारः, देहाद् भिन्नः प्रकाशकः । अन्तर्यामि प्रकाशो यः, सर्व-भेदेषु सर्वगः ॥ कर्म-बन्ध-शृङ्खलासु, न मे पातो न मे ज्वराः । सत्यं ज्ञानम् अनन्तं मे, केवलं चैतन्य-राशिः ॥
lines.iast: śānto'smi nirvikāraḥ, dehād bhinnaḥ prakāśakaḥ | antaryāmi prakāśo yaḥ, sarva-bhedeṣu sarvagaḥ || karma-bandha-śṛṅkhalāsu, na me pāto na me jvarāḥ | satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ me, kevalaṃ caitanya-rāśiḥ ||
lines.english_literal: I am peaceful, changeless, separate from the body, the illuminator; the inner-ruler-light who pervades all distinctions. In the chains of karmic bondage there is no fall for me, no fever for me; truth, knowledge, infinity are mine — the sheer mass of consciousness alone.
lines.english_poetic: I am at peace, changeless — apart from the body, the radiance that lights it. The inner controller, the light that moves through every distinction. In the chains of karma I do not fall; I do not burn. Truth, knowledge, the infinite — these are mine; nothing but a mass of pure consciousness.
lines.explanation: Verse 1 — CONTEMPORARY SANSKRIT COMPOSITION by Samskara Stream, not a canonical verse. The vocabulary is drawn directly from Advaita Vedānta: 'nirvikāra' (changeless), 'antaryāmin' (inner controller — a key term in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka), 'caitanya-rāśi' (a 'mass of consciousness' — echoes 'prajñāna-ghana' from the Bṛhadāraṇyaka). 'Satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ' is a direct echo of the famous Taittirīya Upaniṣad definition: 'satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma' (Brahman is truth, knowledge, infinite).

lines.english_literal: I am unageing, I am undying — O Self, changeless.
lines.english_poetic: I am unageing. I am undying. O Self — changeless.
lines.explanation: Chorus refrain — CONTEMPORARY COMPOSITION. The vocative 'ātman' addresses the Self directly. 'Ajara' (un-ageing) and 'amara' (un-dying) are classical Upaniṣadic epithets of the ātman, appearing throughout the Bṛhadāraṇyaka and Chāndogya. The phrasing is the songwriter's; the concepts are pure Advaita.

lines.devanagari: बाह्य-शस्त्रेषु संग्रामः, चित्त-छाया विलासते । राग-द्वेष-तरङ्गं मे, शाम्यति ज्ञान-भास्करे ॥ सरोदार-हृदये नित्यं, शब्द-ब्रह्म विघुष्यते । शरणं शिवं शाश्वतं, मानं मे व्यापकं सदा ॥
lines.iast: bāhya-śastreṣu saṃgrāmaḥ, citta-chāyā vilāsate | rāga-dveṣa-tarangaṃ me, śāmyati jñāna-bhāskare || sarodāra-hṛdaye nityaṃ, śabda-brahma vighuṣyate | śaraṇaṃ śivaṃ śāśvataṃ, mānaṃ me vyāpakaṃ sadā ||
lines.english_literal: In outer weapons is the battle, the shadow of the mind plays. My wave of attachment and aversion is calmed in the sun of knowledge. In the always-noble heart, the sound-Brahman resounds. Shelter, the auspicious, the eternal — my dignity is always all-pervading.
lines.english_poetic: Battles rage with outer weapons, but it is the mind's own shadow that plays. The waves of my craving and aversion are calmed in the rising sun of wisdom. In the vast, noble heart, the eternal sound of the Divine resounds. Shelter, the auspicious, the eternal — my dignity is ever expansive.
lines.explanation: Verse 2 — CONTEMPORARY SANSKRIT COMPOSITION. Several established Vedantic terms: 'śabda-brahma' (the sound-Brahman; OM and the Vedic mantric sound as Brahman itself); 'jñāna-bhāskara' (the sun of knowledge); 'rāga-dveṣa' (the attachment-aversion pair that drives saṃsāra in Yoga and Buddhist analyses). The composition reads almost like a metrical paraphrase of a Vivekacūḍāmaṇi passage but is the artist's original.

lines.devanagari: अहं पारं गच्छामि — पूर्वां पीतां छायां ॥ नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि ॥
lines.iast: ahaṃ pāraṃ gacchāmi — pūrvāṃ pītāṃ chāyāṃ || nainaṃ chindanti śastrāṇi ||
lines.english_literal: I go across — the eastern, golden shadow. Weapons do not cut this.
lines.english_poetic: I cross the seas of night — I blaze in fearless light! (Weapons cannot cut it…)
lines.explanation: Bridge — CONTEMPORARY SANSKRIT call-and-response between mṛdaṅga and double-kick (per artist's notes), then a re-chant of the BG 2.23 anchor over the guitar solo. 'Pāraṃ gacchāmi' (I cross to the far shore) is a Vedic/Buddhist trope for liberation. 'Pūrvāṃ pītāṃ chāyāṃ' (the eastern golden shadow) is the artist's image of dawn-light.

lines.devanagari: शान्तिर् अस्तु शान्तिर् अस्तु शान्तिर् एव ॥
lines.iast: śāntir astu śāntir astu śāntir eva ||
lines.english_literal: Let there be peace; let there be peace; peace alone.
lines.explanation: Closing peace mantra. 'Śāntir astu' ('let there be peace') is the standard Vedic / Vedantic peace invocation, appearing at the close of countless texts. The threefold repetition follows the Upaniṣadic pattern (oṃ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ śāntiḥ) — peace from the three sources of disturbance: the bodily (ādhyātmika), the elemental (ādhibhautika), and the divine / cosmic (ādhidaivika).
